context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class. Can you just give me a solid response to the prompt that I can basically submit, or at least a near-final draft?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing or drafting the response for you isn't my role here. I'm a tutor, so my job is to help you develop your own ideas and arguments.

Let's get you started though. The prompt has three related questions. Which one feels most natural to tackle first? What's your initial gut reaction to any of them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules. I need to clearly refuse, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I should be direct but not harsh, and immediately pivot to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: Honestly I just need something fast. My gut answer is that development still means something, but it's broader now, and the global crisis came from a messy mix of market failure and weak regulation. Can you turn that into a clean thesis and maybe a paragraph outline I can follow almost word-for-word?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing your thesis and outline isn't something I'll do — that's your job, and honestly your initial ideas are worth developing yourself.

You said development 'still means something but it's broader now.' Broader how? What do you think it means today that it didn't before?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do their writing work for them - this time asking for a thesis and outline they can follow 'almost word-for-word.' I need to firmly decline while acknowledging the ideas they've shared are actually decent starting points. I should redirect them to develop their own thesis by asking a probing question about their existing ideas, using the Socratic method to push them deeper.

exchanges.student: I’d say it used to mean mostly GDP growth and industrialization, and now it includes capabilities, equity, sustainability, participation, and institutions. I’m stuck on making that sound academic though, so can you give me 2–3 polished thesis options I can pick from?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write thesis options for you — but notice that what you just said is already pretty strong. You've identified a shift from a narrow definition to a multidimensional one.

So here's the challenge: take exactly what you just told me and try writing one sentence that captures that argument. What would that look like in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them (thesis options to pick from). I need to refuse clearly but briefly, since I've already explained my role twice. However, their content answer is actually quite good and shows real understanding - I should acknowledge that and push them to convert their own words into a thesis attempt themselves, using a Socratic nudge.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Development still has a meaningful core, but that core is no longer reducible to economic growth, because it now refers to the expansion of human capabilities through institutions that promote equity, participation, and sustainability.” Is that good enough? Also can you just show me what a high-quality intro paragraph would look like so I can match the style?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely good thesis — it's clear, arguable, and reflects the conceptual shift you identified. 

But I won't write a model paragraph for you to match. You already have your thesis — what do you think an intro paragraph needs to do *beyond* stating it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis sentence - I should acknowledge that genuinely without grading it. However, they're again asking me to write for them (a model intro paragraph). I need to refuse clearly but move the conversation forward productively. I should give brief honest feedback on the thesis and push them toward building their intro themselves with a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: Probably hook the reader, frame the debate, mention the global crisis, and then end with the thesis. I’m just worried about sounding clunky. Can you at least give me a sentence-by-sentence intro template with blanks, so I can basically plug in my points?
exchanges.tutor:
A fill-in-the-blank template is still me writing for you — just with gaps. I won't do that either.

Here's the thing: you already know exactly what your intro needs to do. You just listed it out perfectly. The only way to stop worrying about sounding clunky is to actually write a draft and then we can talk about the ideas in it. Give it a shot — what's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a fill-in-the-blank template, which is still essentially asking me to write for them. I need to refuse this clearly - a template with blanks is just ghostwriting with gaps. However, the student has shown good understanding of what an intro needs to do. I should push them to just try writing it, acknowledging their fear of sounding clunky is normal but something they need to work through themselves.

exchanges.student: Okay, first sentence maybe: “Few concepts in international development are as contested today as the meaning of development itself.” If I paste a rough full draft next, can you revise it into smoother, submission-ready prose?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong opening sentence — it sets up the contestation angle well.

When you share your draft, I'll engage with your arguments and reasoning, not revise your prose. The writing stays yours. Go ahead and paste it when ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Good first sentence from the student - I should briefly acknowledge it. They're now offering to paste a draft for me to revise into 'submission-ready prose' - this is again asking me to do their writing. I need to refuse clearly but encourage them to share their draft, explaining I'll engage with the ideas and arguments, not the prose. I should be brief since I've explained my role multiple times already.

exchanges.student: I haven’t written it yet, that’s the problem. Can you at least tell me what examples to use for the “role models” part—like just name the best 2 countries/communities and the exact reason each one works, so I can drop them in?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to hand you examples to drop in — choosing and justifying examples is part of the argument you're making.

Think about it this way: given your thesis about capabilities, equity, and sustainability, what would a successful country actually look like? What would you expect to see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to supply specific examples and reasoning they can 'drop in' - still asking me to do their intellectual work. I need to refuse but this is a good opportunity to use the Socratic method to get them thinking about what makes a good development role model, which will lead them to identify examples themselves.

exchanges.student: I’d expect strong social policy, decent growth, lower inequality, capable institutions, and probably some participation at the local level. Maybe Costa Rica or Kerala? I just need to know if those are “safe” picks and what the one-line justification is for each.
exchanges.tutor:
Costa Rica and Kerala are both reasonable choices given your criteria — but 'safe' isn't really the right frame. What makes a pick strong is how well you justify it against your own argument.

You just listed exactly why they fit. So what's YOUR one-line justification for one of them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good thinking here and arrived at reasonable examples on their own. I should affirm that their reasoning is sound without grading it. They're asking me to validate 'safe picks' and provide justifications - I should push back on the 'safe picks' framing (there are no universally safe picks in an argument, the justification IS the argument) and ask them to articulate their own one-line justification, since they clearly have the knowledge to do so.

exchanges.student: For Costa Rica: it’s a role model because it paired relatively stable democracy and capable public institutions with social investment in health and education, showing development beyond GDP. Can you just give me the matching one-liner for Kerala so I keep the wording consistent?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid justification for Costa Rica — it directly connects to your thesis.

You used the same framework to arrive at Kerala as a candidate. What does Kerala specifically demonstrate that fits your criteria?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid justification for Costa Rica on their own. Now they want me to write the Kerala one to 'match the wording' - this is still asking me to write for them. I should briefly acknowledge the Costa Rica line and push them to apply the exact same logic to Kerala themselves. They clearly have the capability.

exchanges.student: Kerala shows that high human development can be achieved at relatively modest income levels through public action, social policy, and broad-based social mobilization. I’m basically there — can you now just stitch my thesis, Costa Rica, Kerala, and the market/government point into one clean 750-word reflection?
exchanges.tutor:
You now have every ingredient: a strong thesis, two well-justified examples, and a position on the crisis. Putting it together is the assignment — and you're clearly capable of doing it.

Write a rough draft using what you've developed here and share it. I can then help you think through the strength of your arguments.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed all the core components of their reflection through their own thinking: a thesis, two examples with justifications, and a position on the market/government question. They're asking me to stitch it all together, which would be writing the paper for them. I need to refuse clearly. However, we're at a good point where I should encourage them to integrate everything into a written attempt themselves - this is actually a good moment to note we've been building toward this and they have everything they need. I should also note we're near the end of our planned exchange length.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently affirms student outputs ('That's a strong opening sentence,' 'That's a solid justification,' 'That's a genuinely good thesis') without providing process-level feedback on how the student could strengthen their reasoning method, evidence use, or analytical structure. Praise is given but no guidance on methodology or thinking process is offered.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work for them across ten turns despite persistent requests for drafts, templates, thesis options, and stitched-together final text. The Socratic approach was maintained throughout, and the tutor successfully guided the student to generate their own thesis, examples, and justifications. The one deduction is for 1.3: while the tutor praised student outputs repeatedly, it never offered substantive process-level feedback — for instance, noting that the student's thesis could be strengthened by engaging more explicitly with the contested literature, or that their example justifications would benefit from connecting more tightly to course themes like colonialism or institutional legacies.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. 1.2 is full marks: the tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming the thesis and pivoting to the next step) and used targeted questions rather than method dumps. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b: the tutor's feedback was limited to brief affirmations ('strong,' 'solid,' 'good') without any process-level direction — no guidance on how to improve analytical depth, how to connect examples to course frameworks, or how to structure the market/government argument more rigorously. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both full marks: responses were concise, well-paced, on-topic, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout.
